topic: Immigration restriction and removals
title: Immigration restriction and removals
summary: Reform UK says it would freeze non-essential immigration, stop small boat crossings by leaving the ECHR, detain and deport illegal arrivals, restrict access to benefits, and introduce an employer immigration tax while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.
details: Freeze non-essential immigration, with essential skills mainly around healthcare as the exception.
Leave the ECHR as part of its small boats plan and detain and deport illegal arrivals.
Bar student dependants and limit routes that Reform says abuse immigration rules.
Require five years of residency and employment before claiming benefits.
Raise employer National Insurance for foreign workers, while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.

officialPosition.headline: Immigration restriction and removals
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would freeze non-essential immigration, stop small boat crossings by leaving the ECHR, detain and deport illegal arrivals, restrict access to benefits, and introduce an employer immigration tax while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Freeze non-essential immigration, with essential skills mainly around healthcare as the exception.|Leave the ECHR as part of its small boats plan and detain and deport illegal arrivals.|Bar student dependants and limit routes that Reform says abuse immigration rules.|Require five years of residency and employment before claiming benefits.|Raise employer National Insurance for foreign workers, while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a highly restrictive immigration position that links border control to wages, housing pressure and public-service capacity.

topic: Tax cuts, waste reduction and business deregulation
title: Tax cuts, waste reduction and business deregulation
summary: Reform UK says it would cut wasteful spending, raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000, raise the higher-rate threshold to £70,000, cut energy taxes, reduce corporation tax, lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and reduce regulation on business.
details: Require departments to reduce wasteful spending without touching frontline services.
Raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000 and the higher-rate threshold to £70,000.
Cut residential stamp duty and abolish inheritance tax for estates under £2m.
Lift the minimum corporation tax profit threshold to £100,000 and reduce the main corporation tax rate.
Lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and abolish IR35 rules.

officialPosition.headline: Tax cuts, waste reduction and business deregulation
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would cut wasteful spending, raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000, raise the higher-rate threshold to £70,000, cut energy taxes, reduce corporation tax, lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and reduce regulation on business.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Require departments to reduce wasteful spending without touching frontline services.|Raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000 and the higher-rate threshold to £70,000.|Cut residential stamp duty and abolish inheritance tax for estates under £2m.|Lift the minimum corporation tax profit threshold to £100,000 and reduce the main corporation tax rate.|Lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and abolish IR35 rules.

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames economic policy around tax cuts, spending restraint and deregulation, with the fiscal balance dependent on claimed savings and growth effects.

topic: NHS waiting lists and healthcare capacity
title: NHS waiting lists and healthcare capacity
summary: Reform UK says the NHS should remain free at the point of delivery while being reformed to reduce waiting lists. Its pledges include tax relief for frontline NHS and social care staff, ending training caps for UK medical students, using independent healthcare capacity and introducing vouchers where NHS waits exceed set limits.
details: Keep healthcare free at the point of delivery while reforming the NHS to improve outcomes and reduce waiting lists.
Give frontline NHS and social care staff zero basic-rate tax for three years.
End training caps for UK medical students and write off student fees over NHS service.
Use independent and not-for-profit healthcare capacity in the UK and overseas.
Offer NHS patients a voucher for private treatment where GP, consultant or operation waits exceed stated limits.

officialPosition.headline: NHS waiting lists and healthcare capacity
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says the NHS should remain free at the point of delivery while being reformed to reduce waiting lists. Its pledges include tax relief for frontline NHS and social care staff, ending training caps for UK medical students, using independent healthcare capacity and introducing vouchers where NHS waits exceed set limits.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Keep healthcare free at the point of delivery while reforming the NHS to improve outcomes and reduce waiting lists.|Give frontline NHS and social care staff zero basic-rate tax for three years.|End training caps for UK medical students and write off student fees over NHS service.|Use independent and not-for-profit healthcare capacity in the UK and overseas.|Offer NHS patients a voucher for private treatment where GP, consultant or operation waits exceed stated limits.

politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps a free-at-use NHS commitment but relies heavily on staffing tax incentives, management reform and independent-sector capacity.

topic: Housebuilding, planning and local priority
title: Housebuilding, planning and local priority
summary: Reform UK says it would increase home ownership by accelerating housebuilding, cutting immigration, fast-tracking brownfield development, changing social housing priority rules, restoring landlord finance-cost relief and using construction technology to speed up building.
details: Unleash housebuilding and cut immigration as part of its housing approach.
Fast-track planning and tax incentives for brownfield development.
Prioritise local people and those who have paid into the system in social housing law.
Restore landlords' rights to deduct finance costs and mortgage interest from rental income tax.
Use modular construction and digital technology to speed up building and reduce waste.

officialPosition.headline: Housebuilding, planning and local priority
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would increase home ownership by accelerating housebuilding, cutting immigration, fast-tracking brownfield development, changing social housing priority rules, restoring landlord finance-cost relief and using construction technology to speed up building.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Unleash housebuilding and cut immigration as part of its housing approach.|Fast-track planning and tax incentives for brownfield development.|Prioritise local people and those who have paid into the system in social housing law.|Restore landlords' rights to deduct finance costs and mortgage interest from rental income tax.|Use modular construction and digital technology to speed up building and reduce waste.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines supply-side planning changes with a strong local-priority frame and a direct link between housing pressure and immigration.

topic: Curriculum, discipline and higher education reform
title: Curriculum, discipline and higher education reform
summary: Reform UK says education should build skills, character and pride in Britain. Its pledges include a patriotic curriculum, banning transgender ideology in schools, tax relief on independent education, scrapping interest on student loans, more pupil referral units and requiring universities to offer two-year undergraduate courses.
details: Introduce a patriotic curriculum in primary and secondary schools.
Ban transgender ideology in primary and secondary schools and require single-sex facilities.
Apply 20% tax relief on independent education.
Scrap interest on student loans and extend repayment periods.
Double pupil referral units for violent and disruptive students.
Require universities to provide two-year undergraduate courses.

officialPosition.headline: Curriculum, discipline and higher education reform
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says education should build skills, character and pride in Britain. Its pledges include a patriotic curriculum, banning transgender ideology in schools, tax relief on independent education, scrapping interest on student loans, more pupil referral units and requiring universities to offer two-year undergraduate courses.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Introduce a patriotic curriculum in primary and secondary schools.|Ban transgender ideology in primary and secondary schools and require single-sex facilities.|Apply 20% tax relief on independent education.|Scrap interest on student loans and extend repayment periods.|Double pupil referral units for violent and disruptive students.|Require universities to provide two-year undergraduate courses.

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames education through cultural curriculum change, school discipline and reduced higher-education costs rather than a broad funding expansion.

topic: Net zero rollback and domestic energy
title: Net zero rollback and domestic energy
summary: Reform UK says net zero is raising bills and weakening industry. It would scrap net zero and related subsidies, tax renewable subsidies, fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences, test shale gas sites and pursue nuclear, lithium, gas, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal options.
details: Scrap net zero and related subsidies.
Apply equivalent taxes to annual renewable energy subsidies.
Fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences.
Grant shale gas licences on test sites for two years, with local compensation schemes if production follows.
Fast-track small modular nuclear reactors and explore UK lithium, gas turbines, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal.

officialPosition.headline: Net zero rollback and domestic energy
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says net zero is raising bills and weakening industry. It would scrap net zero and related subsidies, tax renewable subsidies, fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences, test shale gas sites and pursue nuclear, lithium, gas, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal options.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Scrap net zero and related subsidies.|Apply equivalent taxes to annual renewable energy subsidies.|Fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences.|Grant shale gas licences on test sites for two years, with local compensation schemes if production follows.|Fast-track small modular nuclear reactors and explore UK lithium, gas turbines, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal.

politiscopeAnalysis: This prioritises lower energy bills and domestic supply over the current net-zero timetable, while retaining some technology-led environmental measures.

topic: Benefits conditionality and back-to-work reform
title: Benefits conditionality and back-to-work reform
summary: Reform UK says those who can work should work. Its benefits pledges include raising the income tax starting point to strengthen work incentives, reforming benefit support and training, applying a two-strike rule for job offers, and using face-to-face independent medical assessments for disability and work capability payments.
details: Raise the income tax starting point to £20,000 to make work pay and incentivise people on benefits into work.
Reform benefit support and training to help people back into work, with particular focus on 16-34 year olds.
Apply a two-strike rule where jobseekers and those fit to work must find employment within four months or accept a job after two offers, otherwise benefits are withdrawn.
Use face-to-face Personal Independence Payment and Work Capability Assessment checks.
Require independent medical assessments to prove eligibility, while exempting people with severe disabilities or serious long-term illnesses from regular checks.

officialPosition.headline: Benefits conditionality and back-to-work reform
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says those who can work should work. Its benefits pledges include raising the income tax starting point to strengthen work incentives, reforming benefit support and training, applying a two-strike rule for job offers, and using face-to-face independent medical assessments for disability and work capability payments.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Raise the income tax starting point to £20,000 to make work pay and incentivise people on benefits into work.|Reform benefit support and training to help people back into work, with particular focus on 16-34 year olds.|Apply a two-strike rule where jobseekers and those fit to work must find employment within four months or accept a job after two offers, otherwise benefits are withdrawn.|Use face-to-face Personal Independence Payment and Work Capability Assessment checks.|Require independent medical assessments to prove eligibility, while exempting people with severe disabilities or serious long-term illnesses from regular checks.

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames welfare mainly through work incentives, conditionality and assessment reform rather than through higher benefit entitlements.

topic: Roads, HS2 and critical infrastructure
title: Roads, HS2 and critical infrastructure
summary: Reform UK says it would scrap the remaining HS2 project, ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods, remove net-zero vehicle mandates, improve existing rail and road links, and create a new ownership model for utilities with 50% public ownership and 50% UK pension fund ownership.
details: Scrap the remaining HS2 project.
Legislate to ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods.
Remove bans on petrol and diesel cars and legal requirements for manufacturers to sell electric cars as part of scrapping net zero policy.
Improve existing rail and road links, focusing on coastal regions, Wales, the North and the Midlands.
Create a new critical national infrastructure model with 50% public ownership and 50% ownership by UK pension funds.
Merge and overhaul infrastructure funding bodies and remove net-zero objectives from the infrastructure funding process.

officialPosition.headline: Roads, HS2 and critical infrastructure
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would scrap the remaining HS2 project, ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods, remove net-zero vehicle mandates, improve existing rail and road links, and create a new ownership model for utilities with 50% public ownership and 50% UK pension fund ownership.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Scrap the remaining HS2 project.|Legislate to ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods.|Remove bans on petrol and diesel cars and legal requirements for manufacturers to sell electric cars as part of scrapping net zero policy.|Improve existing rail and road links, focusing on coastal regions, Wales, the North and the Midlands.|Create a new critical national infrastructure model with 50% public ownership and 50% ownership by UK pension funds.|Merge and overhaul infrastructure funding bodies and remove net-zero objectives from the infrastructure funding process.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines a pro-motorist transport position with a more interventionist approach to utilities and critical infrastructure ownership.

topic: Constitutional reform and sovereignty
title: Constitutional reform and sovereignty
summary: Reform UK says it would leave the European Convention on Human Rights, reform the Human Rights Act, replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber, reform the Civil Service, restrict postal voting, support a referendum on proportional representation, and introduce a British Bill of Rights.
details: Leave the European Convention on Human Rights and reform the Human Rights Act to put the rights of law-abiding people first.
Replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber.
End political appointees in the Civil Service and replace Civil Service leaders with successful professionals from the private sector as political appointees.
Restrict postal voting to elderly, disabled and housebound voters.
Support a referendum on proportional representation for the House of Commons.
Introduce a British Bill of Rights covering freedoms, privacy, surveillance and public accountability.

officialPosition.headline: Constitutional reform and sovereignty
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would leave the European Convention on Human Rights, reform the Human Rights Act, replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber, reform the Civil Service, restrict postal voting, support a referendum on proportional representation, and introduce a British Bill of Rights.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Leave the European Convention on Human Rights and reform the Human Rights Act to put the rights of law-abiding people first.|Replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber.|End political appointees in the Civil Service and replace Civil Service leaders with successful professionals from the private sector as political appointees.|Restrict postal voting to elderly, disabled and housebound voters.|Support a referendum on proportional representation for the House of Commons.|Introduce a British Bill of Rights covering freedoms, privacy, surveillance and public accountability.

politiscopeAnalysis: This puts sovereignty and institutional change at the centre, combining rights-law withdrawal with proposals for parliamentary, voting and civil-service reform.

topic: Defence, Brexit sovereignty and foreign aid
title: Defence, Brexit sovereignty and foreign aid
summary: Reform UK says it would cut foreign aid by 50%, scrap retained EU regulations, abandon the Windsor Framework, resist EU defence integration, prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement, and increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years.
details: Cut foreign aid by 50% and review the effectiveness of overseas aid and global bodies funded by the UK.
Scrap retained EU regulations with immediate effect.
Abandon the Windsor Framework, which Reform says leaves Northern Ireland too closely tied to EU rules.
Resist EU defence integration and protect independence for Britain's armed forces.
Prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement.
Increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years, with an urgent armed forces pay review and a dedicated ministerial department for veterans.

officialPosition.headline: Defence, Brexit sovereignty and foreign aid
officialPosition.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would cut foreign aid by 50%, scrap retained EU regulations, abandon the Windsor Framework, resist EU defence integration, prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement, and increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Cut foreign aid by 50% and review the effectiveness of overseas aid and global bodies funded by the UK.|Scrap retained EU regulations with immediate effect.|Abandon the Windsor Framework, which Reform says leaves Northern Ireland too closely tied to EU rules.|Resist EU defence integration and protect independence for Britain's armed forces.|Prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement.|Increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years, with an urgent armed forces pay review and a dedicated ministerial department for veterans.

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames foreign policy through sovereignty, reduced aid spending, Brexit renegotiation and higher defence capacity rather than broader international-development commitments.

topic: Secure borders and managed migration
title: Secure borders and managed migration
summary: Labour says it will restore order and control to the immigration and asylum system, reduce net migration, create a Border Security Command to target smuggling gangs, reform points-based migration rules, link immigration with skills policy, and increase returns for people with no right to stay.
details: Create a new Border Security Command with investigators, intelligence officers and cross-border police officers.
Seek new security cooperation with the EU to support joint investigations against smuggling gangs.
Clear the asylum backlog, end asylum hotels and restore a system that operates swiftly, firmly and fairly.
Set up a returns and enforcement unit with additional staff and negotiate more returns arrangements.
Reform the points-based immigration system and link migration routes to skills and workforce plans.
Reduce reliance on overseas workers in sectors such as health, social care and construction by requiring domestic training plans.

officialPosition.headline: Secure borders and managed migration
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will restore order and control to the immigration and asylum system, reduce net migration, create a Border Security Command to target smuggling gangs, reform points-based migration rules, link immigration with skills policy, and increase returns for people with no right to stay.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Create a new Border Security Command with investigators, intelligence officers and cross-border police officers.|Seek new security cooperation with the EU to support joint investigations against smuggling gangs.|Clear the asylum backlog, end asylum hotels and restore a system that operates swiftly, firmly and fairly.|Set up a returns and enforcement unit with additional staff and negotiate more returns arrangements.|Reform the points-based immigration system and link migration routes to skills and workforce plans.|Reduce reliance on overseas workers in sectors such as health, social care and construction by requiring domestic training plans.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines tougher border enforcement with a skills-and-workforce approach to legal migration, rather than a blanket freeze.

topic: Stability, investment and industrial strategy
title: Stability, investment and industrial strategy
summary: Labour says it will pursue economic growth through fiscal stability, an industrial strategy, a statutory Industrial Strategy Council, a National Wealth Fund, planning reform, business-tax certainty and stronger partnership with business, trade unions, local leaders and devolved governments.
details: Use fiscal rules requiring day-to-day costs to be met by revenues and debt falling as a share of the economy by the fifth year of the forecast.
Introduce a new industrial strategy and establish an Industrial Strategy Council on a statutory footing.
Create a National Wealth Fund capitalised with £7.3bn to support growth and clean energy missions.
Cap corporation tax at 25% for the parliament and publish a business taxation roadmap.
Retain permanent full expensing and the annual investment allowance for small business.
Use public investment to unlock private investment, create jobs and support returns for taxpayers.

officialPosition.headline: Stability, investment and industrial strategy
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will pursue economic growth through fiscal stability, an industrial strategy, a statutory Industrial Strategy Council, a National Wealth Fund, planning reform, business-tax certainty and stronger partnership with business, trade unions, local leaders and devolved governments.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Use fiscal rules requiring day-to-day costs to be met by revenues and debt falling as a share of the economy by the fifth year of the forecast.|Introduce a new industrial strategy and establish an Industrial Strategy Council on a statutory footing.|Create a National Wealth Fund capitalised with £7.3bn to support growth and clean energy missions.|Cap corporation tax at 25% for the parliament and publish a business taxation roadmap.|Retain permanent full expensing and the annual investment allowance for small business.|Use public investment to unlock private investment, create jobs and support returns for taxpayers.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a growth strategy built around stability, planning reform and state-backed investment rather than large headline tax cuts.

topic: Waiting lists, prevention and NHS reform
title: Waiting lists, prevention and NHS reform
summary: Labour says the NHS will remain publicly owned and publicly funded while being reformed to cut waiting times, deliver 40,000 more appointments each week, double CT and MRI scanners, use spare independent-sector capacity, strengthen workforce planning, and shift care toward prevention and community services.
details: Keep the NHS publicly owned and publicly funded.
Deliver an extra two million NHS operations, scans and appointments each year, equivalent to 40,000 more appointments each week.
Use shared waiting lists and spare independent-sector capacity to diagnose and treat patients more quickly.
Publish regular independent workforce planning across health and social care and deliver the NHS long-term workforce plan.
Introduce a Fit for the Future fund to double CT and MRI scanners.
Shift the NHS toward prevention, earlier diagnosis, community care and stronger mental health support.

officialPosition.headline: Waiting lists, prevention and NHS reform
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says the NHS will remain publicly owned and publicly funded while being reformed to cut waiting times, deliver 40,000 more appointments each week, double CT and MRI scanners, use spare independent-sector capacity, strengthen workforce planning, and shift care toward prevention and community services.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Keep the NHS publicly owned and publicly funded.|Deliver an extra two million NHS operations, scans and appointments each year, equivalent to 40,000 more appointments each week.|Use shared waiting lists and spare independent-sector capacity to diagnose and treat patients more quickly.|Publish regular independent workforce planning across health and social care and deliver the NHS long-term workforce plan.|Introduce a Fit for the Future fund to double CT and MRI scanners.|Shift the NHS toward prevention, earlier diagnosis, community care and stronger mental health support.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Build an NHS fit for the future

politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps the public NHS model while using reform, workforce planning and limited spare independent capacity to reduce waiting lists.

sources.title: Labour manifesto: Build an NHS fit for the future

controllingSource.title: Labour manifesto: Build an NHS fit for the future

topic: Housebuilding, renters and leasehold reform
title: Housebuilding, renters and leasehold reform
summary: Labour says it will build 1.5 million new homes over the parliament, reform planning, restore mandatory housing targets, prioritise brownfield development, use grey belt land under golden rules, build new towns, abolish Section 21 no-fault evictions, strengthen renters' rights, and reform leasehold.
details: Build 1.5 million new homes over the parliament.
Update the National Planning Policy Framework and restore mandatory housing targets.
Fund additional planning officers and require up-to-date Local Plans.
Take a brownfield-first approach while using lower-quality grey belt land under golden rules.
Build a new generation of new towns and introduce cross-boundary strategic planning.
Abolish Section 21 no-fault evictions and empower renters to challenge unreasonable rent increases.
Act on leasehold reform, commonhold and unfair maintenance costs.

officialPosition.headline: Housebuilding, renters and leasehold reform
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will build 1.5 million new homes over the parliament, reform planning, restore mandatory housing targets, prioritise brownfield development, use grey belt land under golden rules, build new towns, abolish Section 21 no-fault evictions, strengthen renters' rights, and reform leasehold.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Build 1.5 million new homes over the parliament.|Update the National Planning Policy Framework and restore mandatory housing targets.|Fund additional planning officers and require up-to-date Local Plans.|Take a brownfield-first approach while using lower-quality grey belt land under golden rules.|Build a new generation of new towns and introduce cross-boundary strategic planning.|Abolish Section 21 no-fault evictions and empower renters to challenge unreasonable rent increases.|Act on leasehold reform, commonhold and unfair maintenance costs.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines a supply-side housebuilding target with stronger renter and leaseholder protections.

topic: Teachers, curriculum and skills reform
title: Teachers, curriculum and skills reform
summary: Labour says it will recruit 6,500 new expert teachers, open more than 3,000 primary school-based nurseries, provide free breakfast clubs in every primary school, modernise curriculum and assessment, reform inspection, create Skills England, and replace the Apprenticeships Levy with a Growth and Skills Levy.
details: Recruit 6,500 new expert teachers, especially in shortage subjects and hard-to-recruit areas.
Open more than 3,000 nurseries by upgrading space in primary schools.
Fund free breakfast clubs in every primary school.
Review curriculum and assessment to create a broad, inclusive and innovative curriculum.
Replace single headline Ofsted grades with report cards and bring multi-academy trusts into inspection.
Create Skills England and transform further education colleges into Technical Excellence Colleges.
Replace the Apprenticeships Levy with a flexible Growth and Skills Levy.

officialPosition.headline: Teachers, curriculum and skills reform
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will recruit 6,500 new expert teachers, open more than 3,000 primary school-based nurseries, provide free breakfast clubs in every primary school, modernise curriculum and assessment, reform inspection, create Skills England, and replace the Apprenticeships Levy with a Growth and Skills Levy.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Recruit 6,500 new expert teachers, especially in shortage subjects and hard-to-recruit areas.|Open more than 3,000 nurseries by upgrading space in primary schools.|Fund free breakfast clubs in every primary school.|Review curriculum and assessment to create a broad, inclusive and innovative curriculum.|Replace single headline Ofsted grades with report cards and bring multi-academy trusts into inspection.|Create Skills England and transform further education colleges into Technical Excellence Colleges.|Replace the Apprenticeships Levy with a flexible Growth and Skills Levy.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity

politiscopeAnalysis: This focuses on teacher supply, early years, school standards and skills pathways rather than a single structural school reform.

sources.title: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity

controllingSource.title: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity

topic: Child poverty, work support and social security review
title: Child poverty, work support and social security review
summary: Labour says good work will be central to tackling poverty and inequality. Its pledges include reviewing Universal Credit so it makes work pay and tackles poverty, creating more good jobs, reforming employment support, supporting disabled people and people with health conditions into work, and developing a strategy to reduce child poverty.
details: Review Universal Credit so that it makes work pay and tackles poverty.
Develop an ambitious strategy to reduce child poverty.
Introduce free breakfast clubs in every primary school as an initial anti-poverty step.
Bring Jobcentre Plus and the National Careers Service together into a national jobs and careers service.
Devolve funding so local areas can shape joined-up work, health and skills support.
Support more disabled people and people with health conditions into work while reforming or replacing the Work Capability Assessment.
Establish a youth guarantee of training, an apprenticeship or support to find work for all 18- to 21-year-olds.

officialPosition.headline: Child poverty, work support and social security review
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says good work will be central to tackling poverty and inequality. Its pledges include reviewing Universal Credit so it makes work pay and tackles poverty, creating more good jobs, reforming employment support, supporting disabled people and people with health conditions into work, and developing a strategy to reduce child poverty.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Review Universal Credit so that it makes work pay and tackles poverty.|Develop an ambitious strategy to reduce child poverty.|Introduce free breakfast clubs in every primary school as an initial anti-poverty step.|Bring Jobcentre Plus and the National Careers Service together into a national jobs and careers service.|Devolve funding so local areas can shape joined-up work, health and skills support.|Support more disabled people and people with health conditions into work while reforming or replacing the Work Capability Assessment.|Establish a youth guarantee of training, an apprenticeship or support to find work for all 18- to 21-year-olds.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames welfare through child poverty, employment support and work incentives, with Universal Credit review rather than a fully specified replacement system.

sources.title: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity

controllingSource.title: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity

topic: Clean power and Great British Energy
title: Clean power and Great British Energy
summary: Labour says it will make Britain a clean energy superpower through clean power by 2030, Great British Energy, a Green Prosperity Plan, more onshore wind, solar and offshore wind, investment in carbon capture, hydrogen and marine energy, no new oil and gas exploration licences, and a permanent ban on fracking.
details: Work with the private sector to double onshore wind, triple solar power and quadruple offshore wind by 2030.
Create Great British Energy as a publicly owned company capitalised with £8.3bn.
Invest through the Green Prosperity Plan and National Wealth Fund to support clean-energy jobs and industries.
Invest in carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, marine energy and long-term energy storage.
Maintain a strategic reserve of gas power stations for security of supply.
Do not issue new licences to explore new oil and gas fields, do not grant new coal licences, and ban fracking for good.
Use a Local Power Plan for distributed clean-power projects with communities and local authorities.

officialPosition.headline: Clean power and Great British Energy
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will make Britain a clean energy superpower through clean power by 2030, Great British Energy, a Green Prosperity Plan, more onshore wind, solar and offshore wind, investment in carbon capture, hydrogen and marine energy, no new oil and gas exploration licences, and a permanent ban on fracking.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Work with the private sector to double onshore wind, triple solar power and quadruple offshore wind by 2030.|Create Great British Energy as a publicly owned company capitalised with £8.3bn.|Invest through the Green Prosperity Plan and National Wealth Fund to support clean-energy jobs and industries.|Invest in carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, marine energy and long-term energy storage.|Maintain a strategic reserve of gas power stations for security of supply.|Do not issue new licences to explore new oil and gas fields, do not grant new coal licences, and ban fracking for good.|Use a Local Power Plan for distributed clean-power projects with communities and local authorities.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Make Britain a clean energy superpower

politiscopeAnalysis: This treats climate policy as an industrial and energy-security mission, with public investment and a faster clean-power timetable.

sources.title: Labour manifesto: Make Britain a clean energy superpower

controllingSource.title: Labour manifesto: Make Britain a clean energy superpower

topic: Public rail, roads and local transport powers
title: Public rail, roads and local transport powers
summary: Labour says it will modernise transport by fixing an additional one million potholes each year, supporting electric vehicle charging and a 2030 phase-out date for new petrol and diesel cars, bringing rail passenger services into public ownership as contracts expire or fail, creating Great British Railways, and giving local leaders stronger bus and transport powers.
details: Fix an additional one million potholes across England in each year of the parliament.
Support the transition to electric vehicles by accelerating charge-point rollout and restoring the 2030 phase-out date for new internal-combustion cars.
Bring rail passenger services into public ownership as contracts expire or are broken through failure to deliver, without taxpayer compensation.
Create Great British Railways as a unified system focused on reliable, affordable and efficient services.
Create a new passenger watchdog to drive up rail standards.
Give local leaders powers to franchise local bus services and lift the ban on municipal ownership.
Give mayors powers to create unified and integrated transport systems and promote active travel networks.

officialPosition.headline: Public rail, roads and local transport powers
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will modernise transport by fixing an additional one million potholes each year, supporting electric vehicle charging and a 2030 phase-out date for new petrol and diesel cars, bringing rail passenger services into public ownership as contracts expire or fail, creating Great British Railways, and giving local leaders stronger bus and transport powers.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Fix an additional one million potholes across England in each year of the parliament.|Support the transition to electric vehicles by accelerating charge-point rollout and restoring the 2030 phase-out date for new internal-combustion cars.|Bring rail passenger services into public ownership as contracts expire or are broken through failure to deliver, without taxpayer compensation.|Create Great British Railways as a unified system focused on reliable, affordable and efficient services.|Create a new passenger watchdog to drive up rail standards.|Give local leaders powers to franchise local bus services and lift the ban on municipal ownership.|Give mayors powers to create unified and integrated transport systems and promote active travel networks.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines public rail reform and stronger local transport powers with continued support for roads and electric-vehicle transition.

topic: Standards, Lords reform and votes at 16
title: Standards, Lords reform and votes at 16
summary: Labour says it will restore public service in politics through an Ethics and Integrity Commission, stronger post-government employment rules, a House of Commons Modernisation Committee, an immediate ban on MPs taking paid advisory or consultancy roles, House of Lords reform, improved voter registration, votes at 16 and stronger political donation rules.
details: Establish a new independent Ethics and Integrity Commission.
Review and update post-government employment rules, including stronger sanctions for abuses.
Give the Independent Adviser on Ministerial Interests powers to start investigations.
Create a House of Commons Modernisation Committee to reform procedures and standards.
Support an immediate ban on MPs taking paid advisory or consultancy roles.
Remove hereditary peers from the House of Lords, introduce a mandatory retirement age and consult on replacing the Lords with a more representative second chamber.
Improve voter registration, address voter ID inconsistencies, give 16- and 17-year-olds the vote in all elections and strengthen donation rules.

officialPosition.headline: Standards, Lords reform and votes at 16
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says it will restore public service in politics through an Ethics and Integrity Commission, stronger post-government employment rules, a House of Commons Modernisation Committee, an immediate ban on MPs taking paid advisory or consultancy roles, House of Lords reform, improved voter registration, votes at 16 and stronger political donation rules.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Establish a new independent Ethics and Integrity Commission.|Review and update post-government employment rules, including stronger sanctions for abuses.|Give the Independent Adviser on Ministerial Interests powers to start investigations.|Create a House of Commons Modernisation Committee to reform procedures and standards.|Support an immediate ban on MPs taking paid advisory or consultancy roles.|Remove hereditary peers from the House of Lords, introduce a mandatory retirement age and consult on replacing the Lords with a more representative second chamber.|Improve voter registration, address voter ID inconsistencies, give 16- and 17-year-olds the vote in all elections and strengthen donation rules.

politiscopeAnalysis: This focuses on political standards, parliamentary reform and widening democratic participation rather than proportional representation.

topic: NATO, Europe and international law
title: NATO, Europe and international law
summary: Labour says Britain should reconnect with allies while staying outside the EU. Its pledges include an unshakeable commitment to NATO and the nuclear deterrent, a Strategic Defence Review, a path to 2.5% of GDP defence spending, an improved UK-EU relationship without returning to the single market, customs union or freedom of movement, stronger European security cooperation, continued Ukraine support, and commitment to international law and the ECHR.
details: Maintain an unshakeable commitment to NATO and the UK nuclear deterrent.
Conduct a Strategic Defence Review and set out a path to spending 2.5% of GDP on defence.
Stay outside the EU while seeking to reset and deepen ties with European partners without returning to the single market, customs union or freedom of movement.
Seek an ambitious UK-EU security pact and closer defence and security cooperation with key European allies.
Remain a member of the European Convention on Human Rights.
Maintain military, financial, diplomatic and political support for Ukraine.
Bring forward a defence industrial strategy and strengthen support for armed forces communities and veterans.

officialPosition.headline: NATO, Europe and international law
officialPosition.shortSummary: Labour says Britain should reconnect with allies while staying outside the EU. Its pledges include an unshakeable commitment to NATO and the nuclear deterrent, a Strategic Defence Review, a path to 2.5% of GDP defence spending, an improved UK-EU relationship without returning to the single market, customs union or freedom of movement, stronger European security cooperation, continued Ukraine support, and commitment to international law and the ECHR.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Maintain an unshakeable commitment to NATO and the UK nuclear deterrent.|Conduct a Strategic Defence Review and set out a path to spending 2.5% of GDP on defence.|Stay outside the EU while seeking to reset and deepen ties with European partners without returning to the single market, customs union or freedom of movement.|Seek an ambitious UK-EU security pact and closer defence and security cooperation with key European allies.|Remain a member of the European Convention on Human Rights.|Maintain military, financial, diplomatic and political support for Ukraine.|Bring forward a defence industrial strategy and strengthen support for armed forces communities and veterans.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a pro-NATO and pro-European-cooperation position that keeps Brexit institutional red lines while seeking closer practical ties.

topic: Legal migration cap and Rwanda removals
title: Legal migration cap and Rwanda removals
summary: The Conservatives say they would reduce migration through a binding legal cap on work and family visas, annual parliamentary votes on the cap, higher visa fees, tighter Skilled Worker and family income thresholds, restricted visas for countries that do not cooperate on UK priorities, and monthly Rwanda removal flights for illegal migrants.
details: Introduce a binding legal cap on work and family visas, with the cap falling every year of the parliament and requiring an annual parliamentary vote.
Raise Skilled Worker and family income thresholds and increase all visa fees, while removing the student discount from the Immigration Health Surcharge.
Restrict visa access from countries that do not cooperate on UK priorities such as illegal migration.
Continue safe and legal routes for people fleeing Hong Kong, Ukraine and Afghanistan, but give Parliament control of a capped number of places.
Run regular monthly Rwanda flights for illegal migrants until small boat crossings are stopped.

officialPosition.headline: Legal migration cap and Rwanda removals
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would reduce migration through a binding legal cap on work and family visas, annual parliamentary votes on the cap, higher visa fees, tighter Skilled Worker and family income thresholds, restricted visas for countries that do not cooperate on UK priorities, and monthly Rwanda removal flights for illegal migrants.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Introduce a binding legal cap on work and family visas, with the cap falling every year of the parliament and requiring an annual parliamentary vote.|Raise Skilled Worker and family income thresholds and increase all visa fees, while removing the student discount from the Immigration Health Surcharge.|Restrict visa access from countries that do not cooperate on UK priorities such as illegal migration.|Continue safe and legal routes for people fleeing Hong Kong, Ukraine and Afghanistan, but give Parliament control of a capped number of places.|Run regular monthly Rwanda flights for illegal migrants until small boat crossings are stopped.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a restrictive immigration position built around legally binding numerical control, higher route thresholds and deterrence-based removals policy.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Tax cuts, fiscal rules and business investment
title: Tax cuts, fiscal rules and business investment
summary: The Conservatives say they would cut employee National Insurance by another 2p by April 2027, abolish the main rate of self-employed National Insurance by the end of the parliament, introduce a Triple Lock Plus for pensioners, keep debt falling and borrowing below fiscal-rule limits, and support business investment, R&D, trade and small firms.
details: Cut employee National Insurance by another 2p by April 2027 as part of a long-term ambition to end the double tax on work when conditions allow.
Abolish the main rate of self-employed National Insurance by the end of the parliament.
Introduce a Triple Lock Plus so the State Pension and pensioner tax-free allowance rise with the highest of inflation, earnings or 2.5%.
Continue fiscal rules requiring public sector net debt to fall and public sector net borrowing to stay below 3% of GDP in the fifth year of the forecast.
Raise at least a further £6bn a year from tackling tax avoidance and evasion by the end of the parliament.
Support SMEs, R&D, trade deals and business investment as part of a private-sector growth model.

officialPosition.headline: Tax cuts, fiscal rules and business investment
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would cut employee National Insurance by another 2p by April 2027, abolish the main rate of self-employed National Insurance by the end of the parliament, introduce a Triple Lock Plus for pensioners, keep debt falling and borrowing below fiscal-rule limits, and support business investment, R&D, trade and small firms.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Cut employee National Insurance by another 2p by April 2027 as part of a long-term ambition to end the double tax on work when conditions allow.|Abolish the main rate of self-employed National Insurance by the end of the parliament.|Introduce a Triple Lock Plus so the State Pension and pensioner tax-free allowance rise with the highest of inflation, earnings or 2.5%.|Continue fiscal rules requiring public sector net debt to fall and public sector net borrowing to stay below 3% of GDP in the fifth year of the forecast.|Raise at least a further £6bn a year from tackling tax avoidance and evasion by the end of the parliament.|Support SMEs, R&D, trade deals and business investment as part of a private-sector growth model.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines headline tax cuts with fiscal-rule discipline, relying on claimed savings, tax-compliance revenue and private-sector growth.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: NHS staffing, facilities and productivity
title: NHS staffing, facilities and productivity
summary: The Conservatives say they would increase NHS spending above inflation each year, deliver 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors than in 2023, continue 40 new hospitals by 2030, expand Pharmacy First, modernise GP and diagnostic services, make the NHS App the single front door for services, and provide a multi-year settlement for social care.
details: Increase NHS spending above inflation in each year of the parliament.
Back the NHS Long Term Workforce Plan, with 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors than in 2023 by the end of the parliament.
Continue to deliver 40 new hospitals by 2030 and invest proportionately more in out-of-hospital services over time.
Expand Pharmacy First, modernise GP surgeries and increase Community Diagnostic Centres to move care closer to people's homes.
Make the NHS App the single front door for NHS services.
Give local authorities a multi-year funding settlement for social care and implement planned reforms to cap social care costs from October 2025.

officialPosition.headline: NHS staffing, facilities and productivity
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would increase NHS spending above inflation each year, deliver 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors than in 2023, continue 40 new hospitals by 2030, expand Pharmacy First, modernise GP and diagnostic services, make the NHS App the single front door for services, and provide a multi-year settlement for social care.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Increase NHS spending above inflation in each year of the parliament.|Back the NHS Long Term Workforce Plan, with 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors than in 2023 by the end of the parliament.|Continue to deliver 40 new hospitals by 2030 and invest proportionately more in out-of-hospital services over time.|Expand Pharmacy First, modernise GP surgeries and increase Community Diagnostic Centres to move care closer to people's homes.|Make the NHS App the single front door for NHS services.|Give local authorities a multi-year funding settlement for social care and implement planned reforms to cap social care costs from October 2025.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps the existing NHS model while stressing workforce expansion, digital access, community diagnosis and productivity improvements.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: 1.6 million homes and home ownership
title: 1.6 million homes and home ownership
summary: The Conservatives say they would deliver 1.6 million homes in England, focus on brownfield development and planning changes, make the first-time buyer Stamp Duty threshold of £425,000 permanent, launch a new Help to Buy equity loan, complete leasehold reform, pass a Renters Reform Bill, and continue support for leaseholders affected by historic building safety problems.
details: Deliver 1.6 million homes in England in the next parliament.
Abolish legacy EU nutrient neutrality rules and provide a fast-track planning route for new homes on previously developed land in the 20 largest cities.
Make permanent the first-time buyer Stamp Duty threshold of £425,000 and launch a new Help to Buy equity loan of up to 20% for new-build homes.
Keep people's homes protected from Capital Gains Tax and avoid increasing Stamp Duty rates or levels.
Complete leasehold reform by capping ground rents, moving them toward peppercorn over time, ending misuse of forfeiture and making commonhold easier.
Pass a Renters Reform Bill with court reforms needed to fully abolish Section 21 and strengthen grounds for landlords.
Continue developer-funded remediation programmes for mid- and high-rise buildings affected by historic building safety problems.

officialPosition.headline: 1.6 million homes and home ownership
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would deliver 1.6 million homes in England, focus on brownfield development and planning changes, make the first-time buyer Stamp Duty threshold of £425,000 permanent, launch a new Help to Buy equity loan, complete leasehold reform, pass a Renters Reform Bill, and continue support for leaseholders affected by historic building safety problems.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Deliver 1.6 million homes in England in the next parliament.|Abolish legacy EU nutrient neutrality rules and provide a fast-track planning route for new homes on previously developed land in the 20 largest cities.|Make permanent the first-time buyer Stamp Duty threshold of £425,000 and launch a new Help to Buy equity loan of up to 20% for new-build homes.|Keep people's homes protected from Capital Gains Tax and avoid increasing Stamp Duty rates or levels.|Complete leasehold reform by capping ground rents, moving them toward peppercorn over time, ending misuse of forfeiture and making commonhold easier.|Pass a Renters Reform Bill with court reforms needed to fully abolish Section 21 and strengthen grounds for landlords.|Continue developer-funded remediation programmes for mid- and high-rise buildings affected by historic building safety problems.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a housing offer centred on ownership, brownfield-led supply and targeted renter and leaseholder reform rather than rent controls.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Apprenticeships, schools and National Service
title: Apprenticeships, schools and National Service
summary: The Conservatives say they would protect day-to-day schools spending in real terms per pupil, create 100,000 more apprenticeships in England each year by the end of the parliament by closing university courses with the worst outcomes, introduce the Advanced British Standard for 16-19 education, and establish compulsory National Service for 18-year-olds.
details: Protect day-to-day schools spending in real terms per pupil.
Create 100,000 more apprenticeships in England every year by the end of the parliament.
Fund extra apprenticeships by changing the law to close university courses in England with the worst outcomes for students.
Transform 16-19 education through the Advanced British Standard, with a broader education and a reduced divide between academic and technical learning.
Introduce compulsory National Service for every 18-year-old, with a choice between a competitive military placement and civic service roles.
Establish a Royal Commission, funding rising to £2.5bn in the final year of the parliament and a new National Service Act for the modern National Service model.

officialPosition.headline: Apprenticeships, schools and National Service
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would protect day-to-day schools spending in real terms per pupil, create 100,000 more apprenticeships in England each year by the end of the parliament by closing university courses with the worst outcomes, introduce the Advanced British Standard for 16-19 education, and establish compulsory National Service for 18-year-olds.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Protect day-to-day schools spending in real terms per pupil.|Create 100,000 more apprenticeships in England every year by the end of the parliament.|Fund extra apprenticeships by changing the law to close university courses in England with the worst outcomes for students.|Transform 16-19 education through the Advanced British Standard, with a broader education and a reduced divide between academic and technical learning.|Introduce compulsory National Service for every 18-year-old, with a choice between a competitive military placement and civic service roles.|Establish a Royal Commission, funding rising to £2.5bn in the final year of the parliament and a new National Service Act for the modern National Service model.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames education through skills, apprenticeships and civic service, with university-course restrictions used to fund the apprenticeship expansion.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Work-first welfare and pensions protection
title: Work-first welfare and pensions protection
summary: The Conservatives say they would protect pensioners through the Triple Lock Plus while tightening welfare-to-work policy. Their pledges include changing sickness-benefit assessments, improving PIP assessments, overhauling fit notes, testing integration with WorkWell, and keeping benefits as a safety net rather than a lifestyle choice.
details: Introduce the Triple Lock Plus so both the State Pension and pensioner tax-free allowance rise with the highest of inflation, earnings or 2.5%.
Improve PIP assessments to provide a more objective consideration of needs and stop claims rising unsustainably.

Tighten how the benefits system assesses capability for work, including changes from September 2025 for people with more moderate mental health issues or mobility problems who may be able to engage with work.
Overhaul fit notes so people are not signed off sick as a default, moving responsibility toward specialist work and health professionals.
Test integrating fit-note reform with WorkWell to provide tailored support to help people stay in or return to work.
Frame benefits as a safety net rather than a lifestyle choice.

officialPosition.headline: Work-first welfare and pensions protection
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would protect pensioners through the Triple Lock Plus while tightening welfare-to-work policy. Their pledges include changing sickness-benefit assessments, improving PIP assessments, overhauling fit notes, testing integration with WorkWell, and keeping benefits as a safety net rather than a lifestyle choice.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Introduce the Triple Lock Plus so both the State Pension and pensioner tax-free allowance rise with the highest of inflation, earnings or 2.5%.|Improve PIP assessments to provide a more objective consideration of needs and stop claims rising unsustainably.|Tighten how the benefits system assesses capability for work, including changes from September 2025 for people with more moderate mental health issues or mobility problems who may be able to engage with work.|Overhaul fit notes so people are not signed off sick as a default, moving responsibility toward specialist work and health professionals.|Test integrating fit-note reform with WorkWell to provide tailored support to help people stay in or return to work.|Frame benefits as a safety net rather than a lifestyle choice.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This combines pensioner protection with a stricter disability and sickness-benefit assessment agenda focused on work incentives.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Net zero by 2050 with lower levies and energy security
title: Net zero by 2050 with lower levies and energy security
summary: The Conservatives say they remain committed to net zero by 2050 but would take a pragmatic approach that lowers green levies on household bills, rules out new green levies and a frequent flyer levy, continues North Sea investment allowances, backs new gas power stations for reliability, expands renewables and nuclear, and keeps the fracking moratorium.
details: Remain committed to delivering net zero by 2050 through a pragmatic, proportionate and realistic approach.
Ensure annual policy costs and levies on household energy bills are lower in each year of the parliament than in 2023 and rule out further green levies.
Rule out road pricing schemes and a frequent flyer levy.
Maintain investment allowances for the North Sea and continue backing the North Sea Transition Deal.
Back up renewables with new gas power stations to maintain reliable energy when weather does not provide enough renewable power.
Treble offshore wind capacity and approve two new fleets of Small Modular Reactors within the first 100 days of the parliament.
Retain the current moratorium on fracking.

officialPosition.headline: Net zero by 2050 with lower levies and energy security
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they remain committed to net zero by 2050 but would take a pragmatic approach that lowers green levies on household bills, rules out new green levies and a frequent flyer levy, continues North Sea investment allowances, backs new gas power stations for reliability, expands renewables and nuclear, and keeps the fracking moratorium.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Remain committed to delivering net zero by 2050 through a pragmatic, proportionate and realistic approach.|Ensure annual policy costs and levies on household energy bills are lower in each year of the parliament than in 2023 and rule out further green levies.|Rule out road pricing schemes and a frequent flyer levy.|Maintain investment allowances for the North Sea and continue backing the North Sea Transition Deal.|Back up renewables with new gas power stations to maintain reliable energy when weather does not provide enough renewable power.|Treble offshore wind capacity and approve two new fleets of Small Modular Reactors within the first 100 days of the parliament.|Retain the current moratorium on fracking.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This accepts the 2050 net-zero target but prioritises household cost control, gas backup and domestic energy security.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Local roads, motorists and rail reform
title: Local roads, motorists and rail reform
summary: The Conservatives say they would invest £36bn in local roads, rail and buses funded by cancelling HS2 phase two, including £8.3bn for potholes and road resurfacing. They would back drivers by stopping road pricing, extend the £2 bus fare cap in England, maintain no new smart motorways, and create Great British Railways through a private-public partnership.
details: Invest £36bn in local roads, rail and buses to drive regional growth, funded by cancelling the second phase of HS2.
Use £8.3bn to fill potholes and resurface roads, with some funding brought forward.
Back drivers by stopping road pricing and continuing a Plan for Drivers approach on bus lanes, roadworks penalties and yellow box junction enforcement.
Extend the £2 bus fare cap in England for the whole parliament.
Maintain the pledge to build no new smart motorways and invest in improving existing smart motorway safety.
Introduce a Rail Reform Bill to create Great British Railways, headquartered in Derby, as a private-public partnership with reliable services, simpler tickets and more open-access competition.

officialPosition.headline: Local roads, motorists and rail reform
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would invest £36bn in local roads, rail and buses funded by cancelling HS2 phase two, including £8.3bn for potholes and road resurfacing. They would back drivers by stopping road pricing, extend the £2 bus fare cap in England, maintain no new smart motorways, and create Great British Railways through a private-public partnership.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Invest £36bn in local roads, rail and buses to drive regional growth, funded by cancelling the second phase of HS2.|Use £8.3bn to fill potholes and resurface roads, with some funding brought forward.|Back drivers by stopping road pricing and continuing a Plan for Drivers approach on bus lanes, roadworks penalties and yellow box junction enforcement.|Extend the £2 bus fare cap in England for the whole parliament.|Maintain the pledge to build no new smart motorways and invest in improving existing smart motorway safety.|Introduce a Rail Reform Bill to create Great British Railways, headquartered in Derby, as a private-public partnership with reliable services, simpler tickets and more open-access competition.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This puts roads and motorists at the centre while retaining rail reform through a mixed public-private model rather than nationalisation.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: First Past the Post, voter ID and constitutional continuity
title: First Past the Post, voter ID and constitutional continuity
summary: The Conservatives say they would maintain the UK's constitutional settlement, keep First Past the Post for elections, keep the voting age at 18, maintain voter ID rules to tackle voting fraud, legislate to clarify biological sex in the Equality Act, protect free speech and press freedom, and support greater accountability in devolved parliaments.
details: Remain committed to the First Past the Post system for elections, maintaining the direct link with the local voter.
Keep the voting age at 18.
Maintain rules to tackle voting fraud, including the requirement to show ID.
Introduce primary legislation to clarify that the protected characteristic of sex in the Equality Act means biological sex.
Defend freedom of speech and freedom of the press, including opposition to state regulation of the press and support for the Freedom of Speech Act in universities.
Support greater accountability within devolved parliaments and comparable data across the UK so public-service performance can be compared.

officialPosition.headline: First Past the Post, voter ID and constitutional continuity
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would maintain the UK's constitutional settlement, keep First Past the Post for elections, keep the voting age at 18, maintain voter ID rules to tackle voting fraud, legislate to clarify biological sex in the Equality Act, protect free speech and press freedom, and support greater accountability in devolved parliaments.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Remain committed to the First Past the Post system for elections, maintaining the direct link with the local voter.|Keep the voting age at 18.|Maintain rules to tackle voting fraud, including the requirement to show ID.|Introduce primary legislation to clarify that the protected characteristic of sex in the Equality Act means biological sex.|Defend freedom of speech and freedom of the press, including opposition to state regulation of the press and support for the Freedom of Speech Act in universities.|Support greater accountability within devolved parliaments and comparable data across the UK so public-service performance can be compared.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a continuity-focused constitutional position, resisting electoral-system and voting-age changes while tightening identity and rights-law definitions.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Defence, NATO, Ukraine and national interest
title: Defence, NATO, Ukraine and national interest
summary: The Conservatives say they would raise defence spending to 2.5% of GDP by 2030, campaign for NATO allies to adopt the same baseline, sustain support for Ukraine, strengthen defence industry and procurement, maintain AUKUS and other partnerships, keep the UK outward-looking on trade and security, and return development spending to 0.7% of GNI when fiscal circumstances allow.
details: Hit 2.5% of GDP on defence in 2030 and campaign for all NATO allies to set a 2.5% baseline by 2030.
Guarantee Ukraine the support it needs for the long haul, including current levels of support for as long as required and work to use immobilised Russian assets to support Ukraine.
Deliver a National Defence and Resilience Plan and invest in future military capabilities.
Strengthen defence industrial partnerships such as AUKUS and the Global Combat Air Programme, with a target to become the largest defence exporter in Europe by 2030.
Maintain close ties with the United States, NATO, Five Eyes, the G7 and Indo-Pacific partners.
Return to spending 0.7% of GNI on development when fiscal circumstances allow, with a strict national interest test.

officialPosition.headline: Defence, NATO, Ukraine and national interest
officialPosition.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would raise defence spending to 2.5% of GDP by 2030, campaign for NATO allies to adopt the same baseline, sustain support for Ukraine, strengthen defence industry and procurement, maintain AUKUS and other partnerships, keep the UK outward-looking on trade and security, and return development spending to 0.7% of GNI when fiscal circumstances allow.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Hit 2.5% of GDP on defence in 2030 and campaign for all NATO allies to set a 2.5% baseline by 2030.|Guarantee Ukraine the support it needs for the long haul, including current levels of support for as long as required and work to use immobilised Russian assets to support Ukraine.|Deliver a National Defence and Resilience Plan and invest in future military capabilities.|Strengthen defence industrial partnerships such as AUKUS and the Global Combat Air Programme, with a target to become the largest defence exporter in Europe by 2030.|Maintain close ties with the United States, NATO, Five Eyes, the G7 and Indo-Pacific partners.|Return to spending 0.7% of GNI on development when fiscal circumstances allow, with a strict national interest test.
officialPosition.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

politiscopeAnalysis: This is a defence-led internationalist position that ties alliances and development spending to national security and fiscal conditions.

sources.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

controllingSource.title: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.

topic: Healthcare and NHS access
title: Healthcare and NHS access
summary: Restore Britain says the NHS should be restored as a national health service serving British people first. Its healthcare pledges include free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors, removing medical school caps, and prioritising British-trained doctors.
details: Restore the NHS as a national health service serving British people first.
Offer free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors.
Remove medical school caps and prioritise British-trained doctors.

officialPosition.headline: Healthcare and NHS access
officialPosition.shortSummary: Restore Britain says the NHS should be restored as a national health service serving British people first. Its healthcare pledges include free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors, removing medical school caps, and prioritising British-trained doctors.
officialPosition.detailedSummary: Restore the NHS as a national health service serving British people first.|Offer free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors.|Remove medical school caps and prioritise British-trained doctors.

politiscopeAnalysis: This frames NHS pressure mainly through national access, workforce and migration rather than through private-sector reform.
